title: High-Impact Leadership Role on the Floor â Demanding Pace
body: High-Impact Leadership Role on the Floor â Demanding Pace
pros: The Guest Experience Lead role sits in a unique and powerful space within the store ecosystem. You are not simply selling product â you are shaping the energy of the floor. You drive the guest experience moment to moment, act as Supervisor on Duty, coach Educators in real time, and translate leadership strategy into execution. The role develops strong situational leadership skills. You learn how to read a room quickly, manage traffic flow during peak hours, resolve service breakdowns with composure, and elevate transactions through thoughtful connection rather than pressure. Itâs an excellent bridge between Educator and Assistant Manager because you gain exposure to performance feedback, hiring input, and operational accountability without being fully removed from the guest-facing side of the business. The emphasis on structure and intentional communication is a major strength. Success requires clarity, alignment, and disciplined floor management â skills that are transferable far beyond retail.
cons: The expectations are high, and the accountability can feel disproportionate to compensation. You are often carrying managerial responsibility without the full authority or pay of a formal manager. Because the role is deeply guest-facing, it can be emotionally demanding. You are absorbing team dynamics, guest escalations, and business targets simultaneously. During high-volume events (holiday launches, Black Friday, etc.), the pace is relentless. In periods of organisational transition, role clarity can blur. Depending on the store, you may find yourself balancing visual, operational, and people leadership tasks all at once.

title: Loved the Team, Lost Trust in Leadership
body: Loved the Team, Lost Trust in Leadership
pros: The team was genuinely the best part of working at lululemon. Very fun, collaborative environment on the floor, and I made friends Iâll have for life. Coworkers were supportive, creative, and great to work alongside day to day. The employee discount was also a huge perk at the time, especially when sale items were still discounted at 75% (this has since been removed). The brand itself is strong and visually inspiring.
cons: There was a significant amount of internal politics, particularly at the management and district level. Our store manager was knowingly managing two stores at once for additional compensation while being fully aware that our location was scheduled to close. Despite this, an assistant manager was brought on with the promise of becoming a store manager, without transparency about the storeâs future. This created false expectations and ultimately felt misleading and unfair to the team. The district manager appeared primarily focused on sales performance and protecting her bottom line, with little regard for employee well-being or long-term impact on the store team. Upper management overall seemed to prioritize profit and brand aesthetics over honesty, accountability, and employee investment.
role: Former employee - Visual Merchandiser Team Lead

title: Good management in the area, company is currently going through changes
body: Good management in the area, company is currently going through changes
pros: I have enjoyed my team a lot. I'd recommend going into each store before applying and scoping out team dynamics and if they actually talk to guests. I did this and love my team I work with now.
cons: Company is definitely going through changes, which is apparent through poor recent performance. Tbh I'm not sure if the current CEO is the man for the job, I don't think he fully understands/grasps the core customer and because of that, lulu is trying to be too many things to too many people. There is definitely an identity crisis right now.

pros: Huge pro was the good quality clothes you had access to
cons: I experienced a huge amount of bullying from my coworkers, and their clientele was extremely racist. It took me years to recover from the trauma of working at that Lululemon location. I have a very driven personality and had a strong retail background when I was hired, but they made sure to make me feel bad for having those valuable skills. At the time, I couldnât even acknowledge how the language barrier and my accent could affect my employment with the company. Of course, that disadvantage was used to harass me and make my life miserable by a group of mean customers and some employees. Although the company attempted to appear inclusive, it was not.

title: Great Atmosphere, Team, Pay, & Environment
pros: *At my location, I had amazing managers who cared about my well-being and the quality of my work. We worked together and made a plan towards career growth in the company. *Pay was great, working weekends and holidays is worth it *The work itself is never-ending - in a good way - if you like an environment that's constantly adapting with the core foundations staying.
cons: *Working in a small mountain town, we had a problem with an older individual who would make micro-aggressive comments, etc, after coming to managers with reasonable proof, they took it up the chain and dealt with it accordingly. They promote a safe working environment and truly enforce it. *If you're on the merch team, be prepared to stay hours after closing. Even at small locations, there's big drop days! But it's doable! Just takes time - which is expected. *Finding a transfer for your specific job title can be difficult, it's a competitive pool!

pros: The people here are amazing, everyone is focused on bettering themselves and the company while making each workday feel like fun. The discounts are great and the added workout stipends is a huge plus.

title: I stay for the benefits

title: Good but could be Great
pros: I love how flexible Lululemon can be, my managers are very supportive of my long-term goals outside of Lululemon and are always trying to help me with things that may not pertain to my life outside of work. I'm able to work with my managers about balancing my schedule to work with other jobs, vacations, emergencies, etc. One thing I can say about working at Lululemon is our benefits are amazing, wether that pertains to the discounts or access to health insurance.
cons: Attendance policies are very strict, it depends on a case by case basis along with how management proceeds with your case. However there is no grace period, getting a tardy removed is very difficult, and even though you may be a great educator they may terminate your contract.

title: Great Place to Grow!
pros: I think lululemon shares a lot of personal and professional development skills, while offering flexability to your schedule. The leaders are clear on their expectations of educators. Educators for the most part are reliable and the community aspect is fantastic. I had recently graduated college and needed a job. They took a chance on me and I feel like I am thriving within the company and in the store. I am connecting the co-workers, creating great guest experiences in a area that isnt percieved to be inviting, and I am learning from the people around me who is what makes the community at lululemon the reason why I stay!

title: fake DEI, nothing equitable or inclusive and not diverse
body: fake DEI, nothing equitable or inclusive and not diverse
pros: entry level pay and bonus. sweaty pursuits allowance to sweat in local boutique fitness.
cons: -Schedule not fair and shows favoritism. -Can't ever request weekend off unless you use vacation. -Wage gap is not supportive to leads or assistant managers. Educators and SM are over paid in relation to leads and Assistant Managers. -Company values being institutionalized (tenured) employees and doest not embrace diverse experience that could benefit sales. Cult like mid-senior leadership - too much off the floor time and some manager roles have limited floor leadership. -Store Managers only work one full floor shift a week - how can you know and lead/develop your team if you only work with the same people on every floor shift. - GEL's and AM of Guest Experience are not supported and are in jeopardy of burnout

title: Work From Home Role
pros: There are many opportunities for growth.
cons: Pay range is determined by city class. Even if you've been with the company for a long time, if you live in a smaller, more affordable area, you're hourly pay range will be less for the same work as others. Growth opportunities have become incredibly challenging to land, due to the climate of the job market. Roles used to be posted internally first, now they are posted externally as well and the company doesn't necessarily look to fill those roles from within. Getting time off is a challenge, not always approved, and has to be submitted at least 4 weeks ahead. Sick pay is available, which is nice.

title: I LOVE my side gig!
pros: I decided I wanted a 2nd job for some extra fun money. I had never shopped at Lulu prior to my employment, but I couldn't be happier. The organization is serious about professional development and its intention is evident in its management. They really want you to focus on providing great guest experiences, everything else, don't worry about it! Most employee just want to do good work and enjoy the perks. For a PT job, we have AWESOME perks...very generous discount, competitive pay/bonus opportunities, generous PAID time off, 401k, and wellness benefits.
cons: The price point of the store has created a customer base that can be entitled and rude/. Misinformation on TikTok has created a narrative that you can return anything used/damage/you don't want for something new. That is not true and we can face the wrath of a guest when we pop that bubble. It can be overwhelmingly busy during the holidays, but thats to be expected.

title: Workplace Culture
pros: Able to sell products you are passionate about, lots of resources for product knowledge and opportunities for continued company knowledge regarding trends, product improvements, etc. Great discount as well.
cons: Lots of theft, little to do to protect employees. No opportunities for growth. Toxic managers and workplace culture with lots of micromanaging. Many leads and managers taking the job way too seriously and holding double standards for their employees. Very immature, lots of people complaining, certain employees not required to follow company standards, while others are held on a tight leash. Don't show up late, put a foot out of line or make a single mistake, you will get written up or given a punishment completely unwarranted under the circumstances. Talks down to employees about their "behavior" yet management displays the same behaviors.

title: Healthy work culture; smart, emotionally-intelligent peers
body: Healthy work culture; smart, emotionally-intelligent peers
pros: Everyone is kind and goes out of their way to help each other. I'm learning a lot about how to be successful in this role, and I feel very well-supported by all of my peers and managers. I've been working professionally in web development for ten years, and this is the best company I've ever worked for.
cons: Highly-complex business processes and technical architectures, but there is a large effort underway to streamline these. In my opinion, we spend too much time on new feature development at the cost of technical debt. This is a pretty normal tension between product and engineering at all my past companies, but the disconnect here feels a little worse than I'm used to. We don't spend enough time working on end-user performance, which has the largest impact on our conversion rates. This is gradually changing though, and I feel optimistic about the future of our tech stacks.

Flexible & Active Lifestyle: Flexible schedules, a focus on wellness, and chances to stay active through company-sponsored fitness events.
cons: High Expectations & Pressure: Fast-paced environment with sales targets and a strong focus on customer experience. Inconsistent Hours: Schedules can be unpredictable, especially during busy seasons and holidays. Physically Demanding: Long shifts on your feet and the need to stay active can be tiring.

pros: Great benefits, they hire awesome people. Great company and team culture in most stores. I felt my manager was highly invested in my development and success.
cons: there is a significant gap between assistant manager pay and store manager pay although assistant managers are directly responsible for 30-60 direct reports, floor management, floor leader development, operational duties, and guest experience management. title: Not what it used to be
body: Not what it used to be
pros: - the company offers good benefits
cons: - overly strict - changed so much in 2 years for the worst - the company had me more stressed than my hospital job - the late policy is trash, you can't be more than 30 seconds late unless YOU are in a wreck or someone passes away. School, traffic, bad weather etc will count against you negatively in the company regardless of scenario - not a good job for students or people who prioritize school over Lulu - required staff meetings that talk about the same 6 topics every month, if you don't go ( even if its out of your availability ) it counts as unexcused absence - The worst thing, selling expensive items to customers when the quality of the product is no longer nice. cheaper clothing brands have higher quality, this did not fit my morals.
